termsSubtitle: Please review and accept our terms to continue using PawBalance.
termsCheckbox: I have read and agree to the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy
tosIntro: Welcome to PawBalance. By using our application, you agree to the following terms.
tosOperator: PawBalance is operated by Hamza Sahin, located at Rotermanni 7, Tallinn 10111, Estonia. For questions, contact hamiissah@gmail.com.
tosServiceBody: PawBalance is a pet nutrition reference application that provides general information about food safety for dogs. The information provided is for educational purposes only and does not constitute veterinary advice. Always consult a qualified veterinarian before making dietary changes for your pet.
tosEligibilityBody: You must be at least 13 years of age to use PawBalance. If you are under 18, you must have parental or guardian consent to use the service.
tosAccountsBody: You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your account credentials. Each person may maintain only one account. You agree to provide accurate information when creating your account.
tosAcceptableUseBody: You agree not to: scrape, crawl, or automatically extract data from PawBalance; use the service for commercial purposes without permission; attempt to interfere with or disrupt the service; redistribute food safety data from PawBalance.
tosIpBody: All content within PawBalance, including the food safety database, user interface, and design, is owned by the operator and protected by applicable intellectual property laws. You may not copy, modify, or distribute this content without permission.
tosDisclaimerBody: Food safety information in PawBalance is provided for general guidance only. Individual dogs may have specific allergies, sensitivities, or health conditions that affect their dietary needs. We do not guarantee the accuracy, completeness, or suitability of any information provided. Always consult your veterinarian for medical decisions regarding your pet.
tosLiabilityBody: To the maximum extent permitted by law, the operator shall not be liable for any damages arising from your use of PawBalance, including but not limited to any adverse health outcomes for your pet based on information provided in the application.
tosTerminationBody: We may suspend or terminate your access to PawBalance at any time if you violate these terms. You may stop using the service at any time by deleting your account.
tosGoverningLawBody: These terms are governed by the laws of the Republic of Estonia. Any disputes shall be resolved in the courts of Estonia.
tosChangesBody: We may update these terms from time to time. When we do, you will be asked to review and accept the updated terms before continuing to use PawBalance.
ppIntro: This Privacy Policy explains how PawBalance collects, uses, and protects your personal information.
ppOperator: PawBalance is operated by Hamza Sahin, located at Rotermanni 7, Tallinn 10111, Estonia. For privacy inquiries, contact hamiissah@gmail.com.
ppDataCollectedBody: We collect the following information when you use PawBalance:
ppDataAccount: Account information: email address, display name, and profile picture (provided via email signup, Google, or Apple Sign-In)
ppDataPet: Pet information: name, breed, weight, age, gender, neutered status, body condition score, activity level, and photos
ppDataFood: Food requests: the name of foods you request to be added to our database
ppDataPreferences: Preferences: your language setting (English or Turkish)
ppDataDevice: Device information: app version and device identifier (collected automatically on iOS for delivering app updates)
ppHowUsedBody: We use your information to: provide and maintain your account; display personalized pet nutrition information; improve our food safety database based on user requests; deliver app updates on iOS devices.
ppThirdPartyBody: PawBalance uses the following third-party services to operate:
ppThirdPartySupabase: Supabase: hosts our database, authentication system, and file storage. All account and pet data is stored on Supabase servers.
ppThirdPartyGoogle: Google OAuth: if you sign in with Google, your email, name, and profile picture are shared with Google for authentication.
ppThirdPartyApple: Apple Sign-In: if you sign in with Apple (iOS only), your email and name are shared with Apple for authentication.
ppThirdPartyCapgo: Capgo: delivers over-the-air app updates on iOS. Collects your device identifier and app version.
ppStorageBody: Your data is stored on Supabase servers. Pet photos are stored in Supabase Storage and are accessible via public URLs. We use a cookie to store your language preference (expires after 1 year). Supabase automatically manages session cookies for authentication.
ppNoTrackingBody: PawBalance does not use any behavioral analytics, advertising trackers, or third-party tracking services. We do not track your browsing behavior or sell your data to third parties.
ppRetentionBody: Your data is retained for as long as your account exists. You can delete individual pet records at any time from within the app. To request complete account deletion, contact us at hamiissah@gmail.com.
ppRightsBody: Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), you have the right to: access your personal data; correct inaccurate data; request deletion of your data; request a copy of your data in a portable format; withdraw consent at any time. To exercise these rights, contact hamiissah@gmail.com.
ppChildrenBody: PawBalance is not directed at children under the age of 13. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children under 13. If you believe a child has provided us with personal information, please contact us so we can delete it.
ppChangesBody: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. When we do, you will be asked to review and accept the updated policy before continuing to use PawBalance.

tips.tip_0: Blueberries are packed with antioxidants and make a great low-calorie treat for dogs.
tips.tip_1: Carrots are excellent for dental health — chewing them helps scrape plaque off teeth.
tips.tip_2: Watermelon (seedless, no rind) is a hydrating summer snack that most dogs love.
tips.tip_3: Pumpkin is rich in fiber and can help settle an upset stomach in dogs.
tips.tip_4: Cooked sweet potatoes are a nutritious source of beta-carotene and vitamin A.
tips.tip_5: Plain cooked chicken is one of the most easily digestible proteins for dogs.
tips.tip_6: Salmon provides omega-3 fatty acids that support a healthy coat and skin.
tips.tip_7: Bananas are a good source of potassium, but should be given in moderation due to sugar content.
tips.tip_8: Green beans are low in calories and high in fiber — perfect for dogs on a diet.
tips.tip_9: Apples (without seeds or core) provide vitamins A and C plus satisfying crunch.
tips.tip_10: Cooked eggs are a complete protein source and can help settle a dog's upset stomach.
tips.tip_11: Plain yogurt contains probiotics that support digestive health in dogs.
tips.tip_12: Cucumbers are over 95% water, making them an ideal low-calorie hydrating treat.
tips.tip_13: Broccoli florets in small amounts provide vitamin C and fiber for dogs.
tips.tip_14: Cranberries can support urinary tract health in dogs when given in moderation.
tips.tip_15: Lean beef is rich in iron and B vitamins that support muscle development.
tips.tip_16: Sardines are one of the safest fish for dogs — low in mercury and high in omega-3s.
tips.tip_17: Cooked peas are a natural source of vitamins A, K, and several B vitamins.
tips.tip_18: Coconut oil in small amounts can improve skin condition and coat shine.
tips.tip_19: Turkey (unseasoned, boneless) is a lean protein that's gentle on sensitive stomachs.
tips.tip_20: Grapes and raisins are extremely toxic to dogs and can cause acute kidney failure.
tips.tip_21: Chocolate contains theobromine, which dogs metabolize far more slowly than humans.
tips.tip_22: Xylitol, found in sugar-free gum and candy, can cause rapid insulin release and liver failure in dogs.
tips.tip_23: Onions and garlic damage red blood cells in dogs, potentially causing anemia.
tips.tip_24: Macadamia nuts can cause weakness, vomiting, and tremors in dogs within 12 hours.
tips.tip_25: Avocado contains persin, which can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs.
tips.tip_26: Alcohol is far more dangerous for dogs than humans — even small amounts can be toxic.
tips.tip_27: Caffeine (coffee, tea, energy drinks) can cause rapid heart rate and seizures in dogs.
tips.tip_28: Raw bread dough expands in a dog's stomach and produces alcohol as yeast ferments.
tips.tip_29: Nutmeg contains myristicin, which can cause hallucinations and seizures in dogs.
tips.tip_30: Cooked bones can splinter and puncture a dog's digestive tract — never give them.
tips.tip_31: Cherry pits, stems, and leaves contain cyanide compounds that are toxic to dogs.
tips.tip_32: Rhubarb leaves contain oxalic acid, which can cause kidney damage in dogs.
tips.tip_33: Star fruit can cause acute kidney failure in dogs, even in small quantities.
tips.tip_34: Moldy food produces mycotoxins that can cause severe tremors and seizures in dogs.
tips.tip_35: Salt in large quantities can lead to sodium poisoning, causing vomiting and seizures.
tips.tip_36: Hops (used in brewing) can trigger malignant hyperthermia in dogs.
tips.tip_37: Persimmon seeds can cause intestinal blockage and inflammation in dogs.
tips.tip_38: Raw elderberries contain cyanide-inducing glycosides that are toxic to dogs.
tips.tip_39: The artificial sweetener erythritol, while safer than xylitol, should still be kept away from dogs.
tips.tip_40: Always remove apple seeds and core before feeding — seeds contain trace cyanide compounds.
tips.tip_41: Fish for dogs should always be fully cooked to kill parasites like flukes and roundworms.
tips.tip_42: Remove skin and fat from poultry before feeding to prevent pancreatitis.
tips.tip_43: Steam vegetables instead of boiling to preserve more nutrients for your dog.
tips.tip_44: Never season your dog's food with onion powder, garlic salt, or other spice blends.
tips.tip_45: Cut grapes-sized foods (cherry tomatoes, blueberries) in half for small breed dogs.
tips.tip_46: Dehydrated sweet potato slices make a chewy, preservative-free treat.
tips.tip_47: Freeze plain yogurt in ice cube trays for a cooling summer treat.
tips.tip_48: Cook all pork thoroughly — raw pork can carry Trichinella parasites harmful to dogs.
tips.tip_49: Peel and deseed all stone fruits before offering — pits are choking hazards and contain toxins.
tips.tip_50: Rinse canned vegetables to reduce sodium content before adding them to your dog's meal.
tips.tip_51: Mash or puree hard vegetables for senior dogs or puppies who struggle to chew.
tips.tip_52: Never microwave bones — they become brittle and more likely to splinter.
tips.tip_53: Introduce new foods one at a time and wait 48 hours to watch for allergic reactions.
tips.tip_54: Blanch spinach briefly before feeding to reduce oxalate content.
tips.tip_55: Remove all visible fat from red meat to lower the risk of pancreatitis.
tips.tip_56: Bone broth (no onion or garlic) can be poured over kibble to add flavor and hydration.
tips.tip_57: Chop leafy greens finely — dogs cannot break down cellulose walls as efficiently as humans.
tips.tip_58: Always thaw frozen raw foods in the fridge, never at room temperature, to prevent bacterial growth.
tips.tip_59: Bake or dehydrate liver at low temperature for a nutrient-dense training treat.
tips.tip_60: Dogs need at least 22 amino acids — 10 of which must come from their diet.
tips.tip_61: A dog's stomach acid is about 10 times stronger than a human's, helping them digest raw meat.
tips.tip_62: Taurine deficiency has been linked to dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) in certain breeds.
tips.tip_63: Dogs convert beta-carotene from vegetables into vitamin A less efficiently than humans.
tips.tip_64: Calcium and phosphorus should be fed in roughly a 1.2:1 ratio for proper bone health.
tips.tip_65: Dogs can synthesize vitamin C in their liver, so supplementation is rarely necessary.
tips.tip_66: Fiber from vegetables helps maintain healthy gut bacteria and regular bowel movements.
tips.tip_67: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from fish help reduce inflammation in joints.
tips.tip_68: Zinc deficiency can cause crusty skin, hair loss, and immune problems in dogs.
tips.tip_69: Dogs are omnivores, not strict carnivores — they can digest starches thanks to amylase genes.
tips.tip_70: Iron from animal sources (heme iron) is absorbed more efficiently than plant-based iron.
tips.tip_71: B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products and is essential for nerve function.
tips.tip_72: Excess vitamin A from liver can accumulate and cause toxicity over time.
tips.tip_73: Water makes up about 60-70% of an adult dog's body weight.
tips.tip_74: Puppies need about twice the calories per kilogram of body weight compared to adults.
tips.tip_75: Protein requirements increase for lactating dogs — up to 2-3 times their normal intake.
tips.tip_76: A dog's daily fat intake should be at least 5% of their diet for proper hormone function.
tips.tip_77: Glucosamine from natural sources like chicken feet can support joint cartilage.
tips.tip_78: Vitamin E acts as an antioxidant, protecting cells from oxidative damage in dogs.
tips.tip_79: Magnesium is essential for muscle and nerve function — found in fish, beans, and whole grains.
tips.tip_80: Treats should make up no more than 10% of your dog's daily calorie intake.
tips.tip_81: A medium-sized dog (10-25 kg) typically needs 600-1200 calories per day.
tips.tip_82: Puppies under 6 months should eat 3-4 smaller meals per day instead of 1-2 large ones.
tips.tip_83: Senior dogs often need 20-30% fewer calories as their metabolism slows down.
tips.tip_84: You should be able to feel your dog's ribs with light pressure — that indicates a healthy weight.
tips.tip_85: Overweight dogs are at higher risk for diabetes, joint disease, and shortened lifespan.
tips.tip_86: A 1 oz cube of cheese for a 10 kg dog is like a human eating 1.5 hamburgers in calories.
tips.tip_87: Measure food portions with a kitchen scale rather than a cup for accuracy.
tips.tip_88: Feeding at consistent times each day helps regulate digestion and metabolism.
tips.tip_89: Working and sporting dogs may need 2-5 times the calories of a sedentary pet.
tips.tip_90: Pregnant dogs need gradually increased portions, reaching 1.5x normal by late pregnancy.
tips.tip_91: Small breeds have faster metabolisms and may need more calories per kilogram than large breeds.
tips.tip_92: After neutering or spaying, calorie needs typically drop by about 25-30%.
tips.tip_93: Freeze-dried treats retain nutrients while being lighter — adjust portions accordingly.
tips.tip_94: Sudden diet changes can upset a dog's stomach — transition new foods over 7-10 days.
tips.tip_95: Giant breeds grow for up to 24 months and need carefully controlled calcium intake during growth.
tips.tip_96: Free-feeding (leaving food out all day) increases the risk of obesity in most dogs.
tips.tip_97: Dogs recovering from surgery may need 10-20% more calories for tissue repair.
tips.tip_98: A visible waist when viewed from above is a sign of healthy body condition.
tips.tip_99: Adding water to dry food can slow fast eaters and improve hydration.
